content.problemStatement:
This is the easy version of the problem. You only need to check whether the given array is balanced.

You are given an array AAA of length 2N2N2N. The array contains every integer from 111 to NNN **exactly twice each**.

You can modify the array via the following process:


Choose an integer kkk (0≤k≤2N0 \le k \le 2N0≤k≤2N).
Choose kkk indices in ascending order: 1≤i1<i2<…<ik≤2N1 \le i_1 \lt i_2 \lt \ldots \lt i_k \le 2N1≤i1​<i2​<…<ik​≤2N.
Then, for each j=1,2,3,…,kj = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, kj=1,2,3,…,k in order,

Move element AijA_{i_j}Aij​​ to the beginning of the array.
The elements of the array are re-indexed to start from 111.


For example, if A=[1,2,3,2,1,3]A = [1, 2, 3, 2, 1, 3]A=[1,2,3,2,1,3] and we choose k=2k = 2k=2 and the indices (3,6)(3, 6)(3,6),


First, the element at index 333 will be moved to the start of the array.

The array becomes [3,1,2,2,1,3][3, 1, 2, 2, 1, 3][3,1,2,2,1,3].
Next, the element at index 666 will be moved to the start of the array.

The array becomes [3,3,1,2,2,1][3, 3, 1, 2, 2, 1][3,3,1,2,2,1].
The array AAA is called balanced if it is possible to turn the array it into a palindrome as a result of performing this process **exactly once**.

AAA is said to be a palindrome if Ai=A2N+1−iA_i = A_{2N+1-i}Ai​=A2N+1−i​ for each 1≤i≤2N1 \le i \le 2N1≤i≤2N.

Your task is to decide if the given array is balanced or not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — denoting that the array has length 2N2N2N.
The second line contains 2N2N2N space-separated integers A1,A2,…,A2NA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_{2N}A1​,A2​,…,A2N​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a string denoting the answer: `Yes` if AAA is balanced, and `No` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e. the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, and `no` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: Each integer in [1,N][1, N][1,N] appears exactly twice in AAA.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The array is already a palindrome, so we just choose k=0k = 0k=0.
Test case 222: One solution is as follows:

Choose k=2k = 2k=2, and the two indices (3,4)(3, 4)(3,4).
Move the element at index 333 to the front.
The array becomes [2,2,1,1][2, 2, 1, 1][2,2,1,1].
Next, move the element at index 444 to the front.
The array becomes [1,2,2,1][1, 2, 2, 1][1,2,2,1].

The final array is a palindrome, as desired.
So, the array is balanced.
Test case 333: It can be shown that no matter what operation is performed, AAA cannot be made into a palindrome.
